text: Hello everyone and welcome to the part three of total mesometrial resection. In this part we are going to demonstrate and explain the entire procedure for performing a parametrectomy. Remember, exposure and removal of the parametrium is a key part for a patient who is undergoing a radical hysterectomy because parametrium is going to be forming the margin, especially the lateral margin for any form of tumor in the cervix or in the uterus which has spread onto the cervix. It is very important to note that approximately two to three centimeters of the parametrium has to be removed so that it forms a sufficient margin because predominantly the lymphovascular invasion, if at all it occurs in the initial stages, it does not go beyond the two and a half to three centimeter mark. The entire surgery is going to be demonstrated and performed with simple instruments like the Bissinger bipolar and the laparoscopic endosharer as you can see. The plane of dissection is more important than the instrument used for dissection and as long as the viewer is able to meticulously understand the plane of dissection, the entire surgical proceedings will proceed exceedingly smoothly.

The surgeon exposes the uterine artery at its origin and applies coagulation, ensuring the ureter is retracted safely away from the artery to prevent thermal damage. The uterine vein is then coagulated and cut, liberating the vascular parametrium. Dissection of the pararectal and paravesical spaces is performed, with careful mobilization and lateralization of the ureter away from the paracolpos. The obturator nerve is exposed and preserved, and further coagulation of the vascular parametrium is demonstrated, taking care to avoid thermal damage to the inferior hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon removes the deep parametrium during the procedure for bulky tumors, ensuring a free margin. The surgery proceeds without a uterine manipulator, requiring strong and constant traction. The anterior parametrium, located near the bladder, is carefully dissected to express and remove the fibro-fatty elements, highlighting the proximity to the bladder and its nerve supply. The ureteric tunnel is visualized as a circle within a circle, with the ureter surrounded by parametrial tissue.

The surgeon identifies a space between the ureter and the adjacent tunnel, exploiting it for dissection. The deep uterine vein is visible beneath the tunnel and is safely coagulated or clipped. The laparoscopic shearer is used for precise dissection, emphasizing traction and minimal coagulation to protect vital structures. The anterior parametrium is successfully dissected out.

The surgeon carefully de-roofs the ureter, ensuring safe coagulation of one or two veins in the area. The parametrium is held under traction, facilitating the dissection and freeing the ureter from the surrounding circular layer of parametrium. This technique provides a large margin of parametrium and ensures the ureter is completely freed from the paracorpus.

The surgeon liberates the fibrofatty tissue from the parametrium, ensuring a sufficient paramaterial margin. The ureter is de-roofed from the parametrium, with traction and dissection being the primary techniques used. The parametrium is grossly liberated, leaving a flimsy band that is easily removed, resulting in the ureter being free from the paracolpos.

The surgeon demonstrates the excised parametrium, measuring approximately three and a half to four centimeters, providing a substantial free margin. Dissection is performed at the bladder pillar where the ureter enters the bladder, facilitated by the completed parametrectomy.
I hope all of you are enjoying the video series which we are demonstrating in parts for the mesometrial resection.
